Vector Geometry

Spherical trigonometry

Picking up where we left off:

Proposition. (a×b)⋅(a×c)=(a⋅a)(b⋅c)−(a⋅b)(a⋅c)\mathbf{(a\times b)\cdot (a\times c)= (a\cdot a)(b\cdot c)-(a\cdot b)(a\cdot c)}(a×b)⋅(a×c)=(a⋅a)(b⋅c)−(a⋅b)(a⋅c).

Consider points AAA, BBB, CCC on the surface of a unit sphere with radius 1 and origin OOO. Let a,b,c\mathbf{a,b,c}a,b,c denote the position vectors of AAA, BBB and CCC relative to the origin OOO respectively.

Definition. Let δ(A,B)\delta(A,B)δ(A,B) denote the angle ∠AOB\angle AOB∠AOB, in radians; δ(A,B)\delta(A,B)δ(A,B) is also the arc length AB^\widehat{AB}AB. We also have a⋅b=∣a∣∣b∣cos⁡δ(A,B)=cos⁡δ(A,B)\mathbf{a\cdot b} = |\mathbf{a||b|}\cos \delta(A,B)=\cos \delta(A,B)a⋅b=∣a∣∣b∣cosδ(A,B)=cosδ(A,B) as a,b\mathbf{a,b}a,b are unit vectors and have ∣a∣=∣b∣=1|\mathbf{a|=|b|=1}∣a∣=∣b∣=1.

Theorem (Spherical cosine rule). For points AAA, BBB, CCC on this sphere, we have cos⁡αsin⁡δ(A,B)sin⁡δ(A,C)=cos⁡δ(B,C)−cos⁡δ(A,B)cos⁡δ(A,C)\cos \alpha \sin\delta(A,B) \sin\delta(A,C)=\cos\delta(B,C)-\cos\delta(A,B)\cos\delta(A,C)cosαsinδ(A,B)sinδ(A,C)=cosδ(B,C)−cosδ(A,B)cosδ(A,C).

Proof. Notice that the dot product

(a×b)⋅(a×c)(a\times b)\cdot (a\times c)(a×b)⋅(a×c)

represents the angle α\alphaα by means of

cos⁡α=(a×b)⋅(a×c)∣a×b∣∣a×c∣\cos \alpha = \frac{(a\times b)\cdot (a\times c)}{|a\times b||a\times c|}cosα=∣a×b∣∣a×c∣(a×b)⋅(a×c)​

as α\alphaα is the angle between the vectors outputted by the two cross products. Using the proposition above, we have

cos⁡α=(a⋅a)(b⋅c)−(a⋅b)(a⋅c)∣a×b∣∣a×c∣\cos \alpha = \frac{(a\cdot a)(b\cdot c)-(a\cdot b)(a\cdot c)}{|a\times b||a\times c|}cosα=∣a×b∣∣a×c∣(a⋅a)(b⋅c)−(a⋅b)(a⋅c)​

with a⋅a=1a\cdot a = 1a⋅a=1 due to aaa being a unit vector. This expression can be rewritten as

cos⁡α=cos⁡δ(B,C)−cos⁡δ(A,B)cos⁡δ(A,C)sin⁡δ(A,B)sin⁡δ(A,C)\cos \alpha = \frac{\cos\delta(B,C)-\cos\delta(A,B)\cos\delta(A,C)}{\sin\delta(A,B)\sin\delta(A,C)}cosα=sinδ(A,B)sinδ(A,C)cosδ(B,C)−cosδ(A,B)cosδ(A,C)​

resulting in the desired cosine rule.

This is the fundamental rule governing trigonometry on the surface of a sphere, and it has interesting implications for what triangles would look like in such a circumstance. For instance, say that the triangle is equilateral: cos⁡δ(A,B)=cos⁡δ(B,C)=cos⁡δ(A,C)=δ\cos\delta(A,B)=\cos\delta(B,C)=\cos\delta(A,C)=\deltacosδ(A,B)=cosδ(B,C)=cosδ(A,C)=δ. We thus have

cos⁡α=δ−δ21−δ2=1−11+δ\cos \alpha =\frac{\delta - \delta^2}{1-\delta^2}=1-\frac{1}{1+\delta}cosα=1−δ2δ−δ2​=1−1+δ1​

which is smaller than 12\frac{1}{2}21​, with α\alphaα thus being greater than 60o60^{o}60o - except if δ=1\delta = 1δ=1, where δ(A,B)\delta(A,B)δ(A,B) and every other angle would be 0 degrees (degenerating the triangle into a point). This also implies that the sum of angles in a spherical triangle exceeds 180 degrees.
